text: Leaves change color in autumn because shorter days and cooler temperatures signal trees to stop producing chlorophyll, the pigment that drives photosynthesis. As this green pigment breaks down, other natural pigments—like yellow and orange—that were masked all summer are finally revealed, resulting in brilliant fall foliage. Smithsonian Institution +2The color changes rely on a combination of different pigments and changing weather: NASA Earthdata (.gov)Yellows and Oranges: Caused by carotenoids and xanthophylls. These pigments are always present in the leaf but are hidden by the overwhelming amount of green chlorophyll during the active growing season.Reds and Purples: Caused by anthocyanins. Unlike yellows, these pigments are not always present. They are actively produced in the fall when trapped sugars react with sunlight and cool temperatures.Weather Impact: A succession of bright, sunny days and cool, crisp nights (staying above freezing) yields the brightest red and purple colors. Smithsonian Institution +3To learn more about the specific biochemistry happening in the trees around you, explore the Smithsonian Institution guide on fall foliage, or read the detailed breakdown on the U.S. National Park Service site.
